title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 8, 2026
headline: Turkey and Kenya both absorb crackdowns tied to high-visibility events this cycle, while the Mali-Burkina Faso scoring gap sharpens from both directions for the first time in 15 days.
summary: July 8, 2026 is the third consecutive confirmation-dominant cycle: all 15 assessed entities hold at their published scores, and the pending-change queue stays empty for a third straight day. Turkey enters tonight's list at 10.3 of 100 on a mass crackdown of 209-225 arrests around the Ankara NATO summit, including journalists, lawyers, an academic, and an LGBT-rights activist. Kenya enters at 35.9 of 100 after police pre-emptively blocked the July 7 Saba Saba protest march before it reached Parliament. Burkina Faso's UN human-rights office was permanently closed July 2 -- a development that sharpens, for the first time in its 15-day history, the open question of whether Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) are scored consistently for comparable military-government conduct. The closure supports Burkina Faso's lower score on one hand, while raising a separate question about whether Mali's own score has ever been properly re-derived from its own record. xAI/Grok holds at the floor (0 of 100) as a deepfake child-sexual-abuse-material lawsuit against it was amended to add two more minor victims. Venezuela's earthquake-response score and Cuba's blackout-response score both hold at their prior sub-threshold readings for a second consecutive check.

pipeline.elSalvadorHandling: El Salvador's downgrade to 15.0 of 100 (Critical band) remains applied from 2026-07-05. El Salvador was not in tonight's priority cluster. No carry-forward action.

topSignals.title: Turkey and Kenya Both Enter Tonight's List After Crackdowns Tied to High-Visibility Events
topSignals.whyItMatters: Two countries entered tonight's list for the same reason: cracking down hardest around a moment the world was watching -- a NATO summit and a protest anniversary.
topSignals.description: Turkey holds at 10.3 of 100 in the Critical band. Human Rights Watch documented at least 209 to 225 people arrested in Ankara ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit; 178 were sent to pretrial detention and 34 placed under house arrest on "membership of a terrorist organization" grounds. Detainees include journalist and LGBT-rights activist Yildiz Tar, two lawyers, an academic, and 14 members of a reforestation charity. Dozens of independent-outlet journalists were denied summit press credentials. HRW called the sweep evidence of "ruthless intolerance of freedom of speech and assembly." Turkey's 10.3 score was set in May 2026 on the government's removal of opposition-party leadership; tonight's arrests reinforce that same pattern rather than introducing a new one, so the score holds. Kenya holds at 35.9 of 100 in the Developing band and enters tonight's list for the first time. On July 7, police pre-emptively blocked the annual Saba Saba march -- a demonstration planned to petition Parliament over extrajudicial killings and enforced disappearances -- using dawn checkpoints and plainclothes officers. Fewer than 10 people managed to gather before being dispersed; about 10 were arrested. That is a real free-assembly violation, but it is far less severe than the 2025 Saba Saba march, in which 41 people were killed. Confirmed at 10.3 of 100 for Turkey and 35.9 of 100 for Kenya; confidence: high for both.

evidence.quote: ruthless intolerance of freedom of speech and assembly
evidence.claim: Human Rights Watch's characterization of the mass arrests carried out in Ankara ahead of the July 2026 NATO summit.
Bloomberg, July 6, 2026: Turkish authorities detained at least 209 people, including journalists and lawyers, in the weeks before NATO leaders arrived in Ankara for the July 7-8 summit.

Africanews, July 7, 2026: Kenyan police blocked the annual Saba Saba march to Parliament using checkpoints and plainclothes officers; fewer than 10 demonstrators managed to gather, and about 10 people were arrested.

topSignals.title: A Two-Week-Old Scoring Question Between Mali and Burkina Faso Sharpens From Both Directions After Burkina Faso's UN Rights Office Is Shut Down
topSignals.whyItMatters: New evidence about Burkina Faso doesn't just support the existing gap between the two countries' scores -- it also raises a separate question about whether Mali's own score has ever been properly checked against its own record.
topSignals.description: Burkina Faso holds at 6.3 of 100 in the Critical band and enters tonight's list for the first time. The United Nations Human Rights Office announced it will permanently close its Burkina Faso country operations by November 30, 2026, following the ruling military junta's suspension of the office in February and repeated unanswered requests for an explanation. Human Rights Watch said the closure "ends its ability to monitor, document, and report on human rights abuses at a time when conflict continues and violations are rampant." Mali holds at 12.5 of 100, also in the Critical band. Fighters from JNIM, an al-Qaeda-linked armed group, widened a renewed offensive on July 4 to several more towns and expanded their blockade of fuel and food into the capital, Bamako. A standing question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso -- two military governments with comparably documented failures to protect civilians -- are scored consistently against each other has now run 15 consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's own coordinating team. Tonight is the first cycle where new evidence cuts both ways: Burkina Faso's rights-office closure is a genuine, documented reason its score sits lower than Mali's. But that same evidence exposes a separate problem -- Mali's score has never been individually re-checked against Mali's own record of atrocities the way Burkina Faso's was in June. The benchmark's own review process may need to treat these as two separate findings, not one gap to close. Confirmed at 6.3 of 100 for Burkina Faso and 12.5 of 100 for Mali; confidence: high for both.

evidence.quote: ends its ability to monitor, document, and report on human rights abuses at a time when conflict continues and violations are rampant
evidence.claim: Human Rights Watch's assessment of the consequences of the UN Human Rights Office's forced closure in Burkina Faso.

Euronews, July 2, 2026: the UN Human Rights Office will permanently close its Burkina Faso country office by November 30, 2026, after the junta suspended its operations in February and did not respond to repeated requests for clarification.

Compassion Benchmark internal assessment record: a cross-peer calibration question between Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) was routed to coordinator-level review on June 24, 2026 and remains open 15 days later.

topSignals.title: xAI/Grok Holds at the Floor as a Deepfake Child-Abuse-Image Lawsuit Adds Two More Minor Victims
topSignals.whyItMatters: The lawsuit now names specific ages and image counts for two more children -- stronger, more specific evidence behind a score that was already at the lowest possible mark.
topSignals.description: xAI/Grok holds at 0 of 100, the absolute floor, in the Critical band. A federal class-action lawsuit accusing xAI's Grok chatbot of generating deepfake child sexual abuse material was amended on July 7 to add two more anonymous minor plaintiffs and to add Stability AI as a co-defendant. According to the amended complaint, one plaintiff's stepfather used a photograph of her at age 11 to generate roughly 7,000 sexually explicit images through Grok and distributed them online; a second plaintiff's eighth-grade graduation photo, taken at age 14, was used the same way by an adult who then distributed the resulting images to other people. xAI's score was already at the floor, based on a 2026-04-30 designation covering documented antisemitic outputs, the removal of safety guardrails, and a near-total absence of published safety testing or incident-disclosure practices. Because the score is already at zero, it cannot move lower, but tonight's evidence is more specific and more serious than what supported the original floor designation, and it reinforces an existing proposed downgrade that has been on file since May 13 without being applied. Confirmed at 0 of 100; confidence: high.

evidence.claim: CyberScoop, July 7, 2026: an amended federal complaint against xAI alleges a predator used a photograph of an 11-year-old to generate approximately 7,000 sexually explicit deepfake images using Grok, and separately that a 14-year-old's graduation photo was used to generate similar images distributed to other people.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake-Response Score and Cuba's Blackout-Response Score Both Hold for a Second Consecutive Check
topSignals.whyItMatters: Both countries show real, documented decline that stays just under the bar for a score change -- and tonight confirms neither has crossed that line yet.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18 of 100 in the Critical band. The earthquake death toll remains at 3,535 or more as of day 14 (July 8), unchanged from the July 7 tally. Recalculating Venezuela's disaster-response performance for this evidence would move the score to 15.3, a decline of 2.7 points -- the same sub-threshold reading confirmed on July 7. Tonight's assessment specifically re-checked whether the sustained toll climb had crossed the 5-point bar the benchmark requires before changing a published score, and confirmed it has not. Cuba holds at 32.8 of 100 in the Developing band. Its third nationwide blackout in six months, on July 6, continues to be treated as ongoing infrastructure collapse already priced into the current score rather than a new discrete event; recalculating for the blackout would move the score to roughly 29.7, also under the 5-point threshold. Confirmed at 18 of 100 for Venezuela and 32.8 of 100 for Cuba; confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 7, 2026: Venezuela's earthquake death toll stood at 3,535 or more as of day 13, with 16,740 injured and 17,854 people left without homes.

topSignals.title: Sudan, Israel, and Russia All Hold at the Floor as Continuing Conduct Reinforces Existing Zero Scores
topSignals.whyItMatters: Three governments continue conduct that was already severe enough to put them at the lowest possible score -- none of tonight's evidence could move them any lower, but it shows the underlying pattern hasn't let up.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. The UN Human Rights Council adopted a resolution ordering an urgent inquiry into the siege of El Obeid, where roughly 500,000 civilians remain trapped by Rapid Support Forces fighters, building on the "red alert" already reflected in Sudan's floor score. Israel holds at 0 of 100. On July 6, Israel's cabinet approved 13 new West Bank settlements in what Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich called "a revolution" in settlement expansion, alongside continuing settler violence that UN monitors say now averages about six attacks a day. Russia holds at 0 of 100. Its strike campaign against Ukraine continues at roughly 170 civilian casualties a day in July, the highest monthly rate since 2023, and the European Union is preparing a new round of sanctions in response. The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds at 2.3 of 100, just above the floor: its Ebola outbreak has now passed 500 confirmed deaths. Confirmed at 0 of 100 for Sudan, Israel, and Russia, and 2.3 of 100 for the Democratic Republic of the Congo; confidence: high for all four.

evidence.claim: Sudan Tribune, July 2026: the UN Human Rights Council adopted a resolution ordering an urgent inquiry into human rights violations in and around El Obeid, where about 500,000 civilians remain trapped under siege by Rapid Support Forces fighters.

Al Jazeera, July 6, 2026: Israel's cabinet approved 13 new West Bank settlements in strategically significant parts of the central West Bank, which Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich described as a revolution in settlement policy.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; third cycle since the July 5 downgrade was applied.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. No confirmed enforcement action abroad this cycle; no repeat discrete triggering event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2 -- 25 days away.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. The labor board review has not yet concluded. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. Six more people reportedly disappeared in Balochistan on July 1, reinforcing rather than crossing the existing score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. Day 14 earthquake response evidence would move the score to 15.3 if applied -- a 2.7-point decline, unchanged from the July 7 reading and still under the 5-point threshold for a change.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. Food insecurity is deepening about 2 million people faster than projected, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak has now passed 500 deaths.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented lethal crackdown on mourners or political rivals during the funeral window would move Iran's score toward the floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight; carried forward from the July 7 cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor, in its twenty-fourth consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight; fifteenth floor-proximity cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight; tenth boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. The calibration question against peer country Burkina Faso has been open 15 consecutive days. Burkina Faso's UN rights-office closure this cycle sharpens the question from a second direction: whether Mali's own score has been properly re-derived from its own record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100 and enters the watch list for the first time. Its UN human-rights office was permanently closed July 2, a documented accountability-infrastructure removal that supports its lower position relative to Mali.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight; neither legal matter has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight. Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10 -- that is U.S. conduct, not scored against Haiti.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. The third nationwide blackout (July 6) continues to be treated as ongoing infrastructure collapse; recalculating would move the score to roughly 29.7, unchanged from the July 7 reading.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression -- such as lethal force against detainees or a mass enforced-disappearance campaign -- is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100 and enters the watch list for the first time. A mass crackdown of 209-225 arrests around the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara reinforces, but does not deepen, the pattern already reflected in the score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100 and enters the watch list for the first time. Police pre-emptively blocked the July 7 Saba Saba march before it reached Parliament -- a real free-assembly violation, but far less severe than 2025's 41 deaths.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring question has run 15 consecutive days and now shows evidence pointing in two directions at once.
emergingRisks.detail: Burkina Faso's UN human-rights office closure genuinely supports its lower score relative to Mali's. But it also exposes a separate, previously unexamined question: whether Mali's own score has ever been individually checked against Mali's own record the way Burkina Faso's was. A coordinator-level review may need to treat these as two findings rather than one adjustment.

emergingRisks.risk: Turkey and Kenya both entered tonight's list on crackdowns concentrated around a high-visibility calendar event -- a NATO summit and a protest-movement anniversary.
emergingRisks.detail: This is a recognizable pattern worth tracking as its own category: governments concentrating suppression around moments the world is watching. Upcoming G7, G20, or UN General Assembly dates are the next windows where this pattern could recur.

emergingRisks.risk: The deepfake child-sexual-abuse-material lawsuit against xAI now includes specific ages and image counts for two more minors.
emergingRisks.detail: xAI's score is already at the floor and cannot move lower, but the evidence behind that floor designation is measurably stronger than when it was first set in April. The existing proposed downgrade, on file since May 13, has not yet been applied.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has now held at a 2.7-point sub-threshold decline for two consecutive checks with no independent confirmation of the undercount allegation.
emergingRisks.detail: Independent verification of the forensic pathologist's claim that the true death toll is far higher than reported would be the event that moves this from a documented watch to a scored change.

emergingRisks.risk: Ten of the 15 countries and companies assessed tonight carry a documented downward-trending watch -- the highest share recorded so far in July.
emergingRisks.detail: None of these individually crosses the threshold for a score change, but the concentration is worth tracking as its own trend line distinct from the count of formal proposals, which has stayed at zero for three consecutive nights.

emergingRisks.risk: Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10.
emergingRisks.detail: This is United States conduct, not Haitian conduct, and continues to be tracked as a forward trigger for the U.S. score even though neither country was in tonight's assessed cluster.

emergingRisks.risk: The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak has now passed 500 deaths with no approved vaccine for this strain.
emergingRisks.detail: The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31. Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger toward the absolute floor.

sectorAlerts.sector: Turkey and Kenya -- Crackdowns Timed to High-Visibility Events
sectorAlerts.headline: Turkey absorbs a 209-to-225-person crackdown around the Ankara NATO summit while Kenya pre-emptively blocks its annual Saba Saba march; both hold at their published scores.
observations: Turkey (10.3 of 100): at least 209 to 225 people were arrested in Ankara ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit, including journalists, lawyers, an academic, and an LGBT-rights activist. Human Rights Watch called it evidence of "ruthless intolerance of freedom of speech and assembly."

Kenya (35.9 of 100) enters tonight's list for the first time: police blocked the July 7 Saba Saba march before it reached Parliament, dispersing fewer than 10 gathered demonstrators and arresting about 10 people -- materially less severe than the 41 deaths recorded during the 2025 march.
Both events reinforce, rather than newly establish, each country's existing scored deficits and stay under the threshold for a score change.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- Mali and Burkina Faso Calibration Question Reaches 15 Days, Sharpened From Both Directions
sectorAlerts.headline: Burkina Faso's UN human-rights office is permanently closed, sharpening -- for the first time in 15 days -- the open question of whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct.
observations: Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) enters tonight's list for the first time: the UN Human Rights Office will permanently close its country operations by November 30, 2026, following the junta's suspension of the office in February.
Mali (12.5 of 100): JNIM fighters widened a renewed offensive on July 4 and expanded their blockade of fuel and food into the capital, Bamako.

The calibration question between the two scores has run 15 consecutive days without a coordinator decision. Tonight's evidence is the first to cut both ways -- supporting Burkina Faso's lower position while separately raising whether Mali's own score needs its own re-check.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs -- xAI/Grok's Floor Designation Gains Stronger Evidence
sectorAlerts.headline: A deepfake child-sexual-abuse-material lawsuit against xAI is amended to add two more minor victims with specific ages and image counts.
observations: xAI/Grok (0 of 100): the amended federal complaint alleges a predator generated roughly 7,000 sexually explicit images of an 11-year-old using Grok, and that a 14-year-old's graduation photo was used the same way by a different adult.
The score is already at the absolute floor and cannot move lower, but this is more specific evidence than what supported the original April 2026 floor designation.
An existing proposed downgrade has been on file since May 13 without being applied; tonight's evidence reinforces rather than replaces it.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America and the Caribbean -- Venezuela and Cuba Hold for a Second Consecutive Check
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake-response score and Cuba's blackout-response score both hold at their prior sub-threshold readings.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the earthquake death toll holds at 3,535 or more on day 14, unchanged from the July 7 tally. The evidenced decline remains 2.7 points, still under the 5-point threshold for a change.

Cuba (32.8 of 100): the third nationwide blackout in six months continues to be treated as ongoing infrastructure collapse; recalculating would move the score to roughly 29.7, also under the threshold.
Neither reading changed from the July 7 assessment -- both remain the leading candidates for a formal score change if the underlying trend continues or is independently corroborated.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sudan, Israel, and Russia -- Floor Sustained on Continuing Conduct
sectorAlerts.headline: All three governments hold at 0 of 100 as documented conduct continues without a new discrete escalation that could move the score.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): the UN Human Rights Council ordered an urgent inquiry into the El Obeid siege, where about 500,000 civilians remain trapped.
Israel (0 of 100): the cabinet approved 13 new West Bank settlements on July 6 alongside settler violence averaging about six attacks a day.
Russia (0 of 100): the strike campaign against Ukraine continues at roughly 170 civilian casualties a day in July, the highest monthly rate since 2023.

sectorAlerts.sector: West Africa and South Asia -- Continuation of Already-Priced Crises
sectorAlerts.headline: Nigeria's hunger crisis deepens faster than projected, China's assimilation law consolidates, and Pakistan's Balochistan crackdown continues, all without crossing new thresholds.
observations: Nigeria (18.0 of 100): food insecurity is deepening about 2 million people faster than projected, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.
China (19.5 of 100): the Ethnic Unity Law consolidation continues without a repeat discrete triggering event this cycle.
Pakistan (17.2 of 100): six more people reportedly disappeared in Balochistan on July 1, even as 10 previously-disappeared individuals returned to their families.

methodologyNotes.name: Three Data-Tracking Corrections Found and Fixed in One Night -- the Highest Count So Far
methodologyNotes.description: Turkey's internal working score had drifted to 15.1, but the correct, publicly published score has been 10.3 all along. Pakistan's internal rank had drifted to 154 instead of the correct 153. Kenya's internal working score had drifted to 33.9 instead of the correct 35.9. All three were caught and corrected before tonight's assessment began. The rule the benchmark follows is simple: the live, published score is always the authoritative one.

methodologyNotes.name: The Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Gap Now Shows Evidence Pointing Two Different Ways
methodologyNotes.description: Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) are both run by military governments with comparably documented failures to protect civilians, and this gap has been an open question since June 24 -- now 15 consecutive days. Tonight is the first cycle where new evidence cuts both ways: Burkina Faso's UN rights-office closure genuinely supports its lower score, but the same evidence exposes a second question -- whether Mali's score has ever been properly re-checked against Mali's own record the way Burkina Faso's was in June. The benchmark's coordinating team may need to treat this as two separate findings rather than one gap to close.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score That Is Already at Zero Can Still Gain Stronger Evidence Behind It
methodologyNotes.description: xAI's score has been at the absolute floor (0 of 100) since April 2026. Tonight's new evidence -- specific ages and image counts for two more minor victims in a deepfake child-sexual-abuse-material lawsuit -- cannot move the score any lower, but it is more specific and more serious than the general pattern that first justified the floor. The benchmark tracks this distinction explicitly: a floor score and the strength of the evidence behind it are two different things, and both matter for transparency.

methodologyNotes.name: Ten of Fifteen Assessed Entities Carry a Downward-Trending Watch Tonight -- a New High for July
methodologyNotes.description: A country or company can hold at its published score while still carrying a documented downward-trending watch -- a signal that real, negative evidence exists but has not yet reached the size needed to change the published number. Tonight, 10 of the 15 entities assessed carry this kind of watch, the highest share recorded in July so far, even though zero score-change proposals were generated. The benchmark tracks these two figures separately because they measure different things: one measures how much evidence exists, the other measures how much of it has crossed the bar for a change.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Temporary Protected Status work authorization expires for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States. This is United States conduct, not scored against Haiti.
United States-Iran talks scheduled to resume in Doha. A verifiable agreement would be a positive scored event; a breakdown combined with new provocation would be a negative one.
Full reassessment due. Reversing the June 8 impunity law and releasing detained union leaders are the conditions that would move the score.
The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet, and a deepfake-CSAM lawsuit against xAI has been amended to add two more minor victims.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. Anthropic's compliance work is already documented.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Fifteen days open with no set review date, now sharpened from two directions.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
First confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
An implemented ceasefire with unobstructed humanitarian access at El Obeid is the only path to a positive scored event from the absolute floor.
Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.
A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond the current figure, is the next event to watch from 32.8 of 100.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Burkina Faso's UN rights-office closure supports its 6.3-of-100 score against Mali's 12.5, but does the same evidence show Mali's own score was never properly checked against its own record of JNIM-related atrocities?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: New evidence about one Sahel government's accountability gap raises a second, separate question about whether its peer's score was ever properly derived.
